Microsoft  ·  GitHub Copilot  ·  Databricks
GitHub Copilot Azure Databricks An AI-assisted delivery walkthrough

From raw data to a live product,
driven by natural language.

A two-part walkthrough of AI-assisted delivery: an agent builds a governed Formula 1 lakehouse, then a branded analytics web app on top of it, every action issued through conversation, no clicks in the UI.

01AI-Assisted Data EngineeringRaw CSVs → medallion lakehouse → AI/BI
02AI-Assisted Web App DevelopmentReact + Azure SWA + Genie chat
Agenda

What we'll walk through today

  1. 01

    Part 1, Data Engineering

    60 raw Formula 1 CSVs become a production-ready Bronze → Silver → Gold medallion lakehouse with a snowflake dimensional model and a published AI/BI dashboard, orchestrated by GitHub Copilot over the Databricks MCP server.

  2. 02

    Part 2, Web App Development

    That same warehouse gets a branded Azure Static Web App front end, four embedded dashboards plus an in-app Genie chat panel, scaffolded, secured, and shipped to Azure entirely from the Copilot CLI.

The through-line

One agent. One conversational interface. The output of Part 1 is the foundation for Part 2, the same pattern of plan → autopilot → visible verification repeated at two layers of the stack.

Why this matters

One agent, the full journey: from raw data to a working app

Understand your data

Point the agent at raw, messy files and it profiles, explains, and surfaces what's actually there, before a line of pipeline code is written.

Engineer the pipelines

From that understanding, the agent designs and builds the Bronze → Silver → Gold transformations that turn raw data into governed, query-ready tables.

Build apps on that data

The same agent stands up a web app, embedded dashboards and an in-app Genie chat, directly on the warehouse it just built, so the data reaches end users.

The honest part: this isn't magic, it's iteration

You'll watch the agent hit real errors, dead-ends, and a wrong answer or two, that's the point. What makes it trustworthy isn't that it's flawless; it's that it diagnoses, adapts, problem-solves, and takes correction, out in the open. We kept the rough edges in so you can see exactly how it behaves on real data.

01

AI-Assisted Data Engineering

Building a Formula 1 medallion lakehouse, Bronze → Silver → Gold with a full snowflake dimensional model, from 60 messy CSVs, entirely through conversation.

Part 1 · The starting point

The challenge: 14 seasons of messy, drifting data

  • 60 raw CSV files spanning F1 seasons 2013–2026
  • Inconsistent everything , column names, conventions, and schema drift across seasons
  • Mixed sources , race results, qualifying, sprints, drivers, teams, calendars, "Driver of the Day" votes
  • Dirty strings , 70+ team-name variants, typos, historical renames

The goal

Land all of it in Databricks Unity Catalog as a medallion architecture, Bronze → Silver → Gold, culminating in a snowflake dimensional model ready for BI and analytics.

Unity CatalogDelta LakeServerless SparkSnowflake schema
60
raw CSV files ingested
14
seasons of coverage
8
planned steps, tracked end-to-end
Part 1 · The toolchain

What the agent had its hands on

GitHub Copilot CLI
The conversational agent in the terminal, orchestrating the entire workflow.
Databricks AI Dev Kit
Field Engineering's open toolkit (databricks-solutions/ai-dev-kit) that installs the MCP server plus a library of 25+ task-specific skills with one command.
Databricks MCP server
Installed by the AI Dev Kit, direct, governed workspace access: SQL, PySpark, UC objects, volumes, warehouses, dashboards.
Unity Catalog + Delta
Governance layer and open table format backing every Bronze, Silver, and Gold table.
Serverless compute
On-demand Spark with no cluster management; every PySpark transformation ran here.
Live web research
web_fetch against Wikipedia and a public F1 API to validate and enrich data.
Session plan + SQLite tracker
An 8-step plan with dependencies kept the agent oriented across many turns.
Part 1 · The toolchain

Skills: reusable, portable expertise for any AI agent

A skill is a small instruction file (SKILL.md) — a reusable playbook that teaches an AI coding agent how to do one task the right way: the patterns, the guardrails, and working code. Skills are a general agent capability — portable, version-controlled, and auto-loaded when your request matches, so the agent follows a vetted recipe instead of guessing. Databricks ships a library of them so the agent applies Databricks' own best practices; the ones below are what powered today's build.

databricks-spark-declarative-pipelines
Builds Lakeflow pipelines, streaming tables, CDC, SCD Type 2, Auto Loader, for medallion ingestion.
databricks-aibi-dashboards
Creates AI/BI (Lakeview) dashboards, and forces SQL validation before every deploy.
databricks-genie
Creates and queries Genie Spaces for natural-language SQL exploration.
databricks-apps-python
Builds Databricks Apps and web front-ends, AppKit, Streamlit, Dash, FastAPI.
databricks-jobs
Creates, schedules, and monitors multi-task job DAGs.
databricks-unity-catalog
Governance, system tables (audit, lineage, billing), and volume file operations.
In this demo

The agent leaned on the pipelines, dashboards, and apps skills to go from 60 raw CSVs to a shipped web app, the same kit, two layers of the stack. Install is one line: curl -sL .../install.sh | bash.

Part 1 · Profile before you planLIVE

Read the data before designing the model

Before designing a single table, Copilot makes one read-only pass over all 60 raw CSVs, locally with pandas, no Spark or Databricks, and writes DISCOVERY.md, the findings doc that plan mode is then built from.

① Input

60 raw CSVs

  • 9 source families, 14 seasons
  • Inconsistent, drifting schemas
② Read-only profile

Local pandas pass

  • No Spark, Databricks, or MCP
  • Observe, don't design yet
③ Output

DISCOVERY.md

  • Six sections of findings
  • Reviewed before plan mode
What that single pass surfaced
  • Filename collisions , 2021 sprintQualifying actually holds sprint-race columns, naive routing leaks families
  • Schema drift , race_results churns 14 column variants across 14 seasons
  • Join-key landmines , power-unit suffixes fragment a constructor, plus an Astom Martin typo
  • Honest surprises , 2026 is a partial season, 2019 ships tracks instead of calendar
Part 1 · Architecture

The medallion architecture

Bronze · Raw

Land as-is

  • 60 CSVs → 9 source types
  • Schema merge across seasons
  • Metadata + sanitized columns
  • All values as STRING
Silver · Clean

Type & normalize

  • Dates, lap times, gaps parsed
  • Safe try_cast() everywhere
  • Team-name resolution join
  • 8 conformed tables
Gold · Model

Snowflake schema

  • 7 dimensions + bridge
  • 5 fact tables
  • dim_power_units sub-dim
  • FK-resolved star
AI/BI · Serve

Published dashboard

  • Validated SQL widgets
  • Lakeview JSON authored
  • Wins YoY, all-time leaders
  • Live in the workspace
Plan mode first

With DISCOVERY.md in hand, the agent drafted the medallion plan and persisted an 8-step todo list with dependencies. The plan was reviewed and approved before execution, full visibility, no micromanagement.

Part 1 · Build, phases 0–2

Planning, infrastructure, and Bronze ingestion

0
Planning
Drafted the snowflake gold schema from the profile and persisted an 8-step plan with tracked todos.
1
Infrastructure setup
OAuth login, created catalog formula1, schemas, volumes, and a SQL warehouse. When the SDK hit a Default-Storage limit, the agent pivoted to a SQL-based path on its own.
2
Bronze ingestion
Categorized 60 CSVs into 9 folders, uploaded in parallel, wrote a PySpark notebook with schema merging and metadata columns.
Copilot callout · self-healing on real errors

Every runtime throws its own errors — the point isn't the specific failures, it's that the agent reads the actual error trace, diagnoses the cause, and repairs it without human help, then keeps going. Here Bronze ingestion hit several platform guardrails (e.g. input_file_name() blocked under UC, invalid column chars, empty-string casts); the agent fixed each with a targeted edit on the live trace, iterating to green in about five minutes, no stack traces copy-pasted into a search engine.

Part 1 · The agent at work

One conversation, the whole build

vscode.dev · GitHub Copilot
plan → autopilot → verify. The Silver→Gold star-schema notebook, authored in VS Code for the Web through natural language, with no clicks in the Databricks UI.

The part everyone dreads: dirty string normalization

  • Power-unit suffixes , "McLaren Mercedes", "Williams Renault"
  • Historical renames , Toro Rosso → AlphaTauri → RB → Racing Bulls
  • Source typos , "Aston Martib", "Williams Meredes"
  • Pre-2021 teams , never appear in the modern teams table

What Copilot did

Built an auditable team_name_lookup using a longest-prefix match plus a historical-alias dictionary. When the user flagged "Cadillac Ferrari" as wrong, the agent used live web research (Wikipedia) to validate every ambiguous team, then updated the logic.

70+
raw team-name variants
→
18
canonical teams · zero null power units
Part 1 · Phases 4–5

Silver transformation & the Gold snowflake schema

Silver, one PySpark job

Reads every Bronze table, types and normalizes, writes 8 conformed Delta tables in a single serverless run.

to_date()try_cast()COALESCEstack()lap-time UDF

Gold, snowflake model

Dimensions, bridge, and facts built in three serverless stages with FK resolution.

7 dimensions1 bridge5 fact tablesdim_power_units
Copilot callout · diagnosing a 100% null join

The first fact build had every round_key null, race results use short names ("Bahrain"), circuits use full names ("Bahrain International Circuit"). The agent diagnosed the mismatch, built a track_to_circuit_lookup, and mapped 15 special GP names by hand (e.g. "70th Anniversary" → Silverstone).

Part 1 · Governed by design

Unity Catalog, fully governed

adb-7405609165901633 · formula1.gold
formula1.gold: the snowflake dimensional model the agent built: dimension, fact, bridge, and view objects, all registered and discoverable in Unity Catalog.

"How hard would it be to fix this from the web?"

Validation surfaced an honest gap: ~50% of race-result rows had a null round_key because the calendar CSVs only covered 2019–2026. The agent flagged it as "expected" and moved on, until the user pushed back.

50%
null round_key (2,971 of 5,609 rows)
→
0%
null round_key after enrichment
AI-executed gap closure

Once directed, the agent ran it end-to-end: found a free public API (Jolpica/Ergast), profiled Silver against the lookup, fixed data-quality issues, added missing circuits, and inserted 119 calendar rows for 2013–2018. The lesson: tighten the planning prompt so the agent proposes remediation, not just reports gaps.

A live AI/BI dashboard, and a clean foundation

  • 4 SQL queries validated in parallel before anything was added to the dashboard
  • Lakeview JSON authored & published via a single MCP call, wins YoY, all-time leaders
  • Built on Silver for full coverage , raw name strings give complete 2013–2026 history
  • Every notebook on disk , reviewable, re-runnable, asset-bundle ready
5,481
fact rows · 0% null FK
13
gold tables in the star
100%
round-key join success, calendar seasons
Part 1 · The result

A live AI/BI dashboard

adb-7405609165901633 · F1 TCC – Overview
F1 Season Overview: a published Databricks AI/BI dashboard rendering real charts straight off the Gold layer the agent created.
02

AI-Assisted Web App Development

Turning the lakehouse into a branded product: an Azure Static Web App that embeds four AI/BI dashboards and a Genie conversational panel, built and shipped from the Copilot CLI.

Part 2 · The goal

The F1 Race Telemetry Command Center

  • A branded Azure Static Web App over the existing F1 medallion warehouse
  • Four embedded AI/BI dashboards , Overview, Race Pace, Constructors, Drivers
  • An in-app Genie chat panel , conversational analytics, no Databricks login required
  • Infra-as-code + CI/CD , Bicep and azd, shipped to Azure

Builds directly on Part 1

The formula1.gold.* star schema, the Genie space, and the SQL warehouse from the data-engineering session are the foundation every dashboard and the chat proxy run against.

Part 2 · The stack

Modern, Azure-native, infra-as-code

React 18 + TypeScript + Vite

Frontend framework and bundler; clean npm run build on the first try.

Azure Static Web Apps

Hosting plus a SWA Managed Function (Node 20) for the server-side Genie proxy.

Tailwind CSS

F1-themed dark palette, red #E10600 / paddock black / chequer white.

Bicep + azd

One-shot provision & deploy; build-time URLs surfaced as outputs, secrets as app settings.

GitHub Actions

static-web-apps-deploy@v1, main deploys and automatic PR preview environments.

Playwright + Vitest

Visual verification (Edge) of every dashboard and route; unit test for the API function.

Part 2 · Architecture

How the pieces fit, and where secrets live

Part 2 · Phases 1–3

Dashboards, scaffold, and F1-named components

1
Author 4 AI/BI dashboards
Inspected the schema, validated every query in parallel, posted dashboard JSON. A first pass shipped broken, caught by a Playwright screenshot.
2
Scaffold the SWA project
Vite + React + TypeScript + Tailwind config emitted in one parallel batch; npm install + build clean on the first try.
3
Components named after F1 concepts
PitWall (top nav), RaceControl (left rail), Telemetry (iframe wrapper), signposts for narration.
Copilot callout · the visible-feedback loop

All four dashboard calls returned success: true while every chart said "No data." Only the Playwright (Edge) screenshot caught it. The agent used the screenshot as ground truth and rebuilt all four in parallel: agent → action → screenshot → agent.

Part 2 · Shipped to Azure

The deployed Command Center

agreeable-pond-0710b1b0f.7.azurestaticapps.net
F1 Race Telemetry Command Center: the branded Azure Static Web App, embedding the AI/BI dashboard inside its own product shell.
Part 2 · Phases 4–5

Genie in-app chat, solving the auth problem

The problem

Databricks Genie's /embed/genie/{id} URL authenticates via the browser's Databricks session cookie. An anonymous SWA visitor has none, so an iframe just returns a login page (403).

The solution

A custom F1-themed chat panel (AskGenie.tsx) backed by a server-side /api/genie-chat function. It starts or continues a conversation, polls until complete, and returns plain JSON, session stays entirely server-side.

Copilot callout · conversation continuity

The browser keeps a conversationId in React state and passes it back on each follow-up, so Genie builds context across turns, just like the native UI. "New" clears it and starts fresh.

Part 2 · Conversational analytics

Genie, answering live

agreeable-pond-0710b1b0f.7.azurestaticapps.net/ask-genie
In-app Genie chat: natural-language questions answered against the warehouse through a server-side proxy, with no Databricks login for the end user.
Part 2 · Phases 6–8

Secure, provision, and ship

6
CSP headers
staticwebapp.config.json permits dashboard iframes only from Databricks domains; a swapped-in malicious URL is blocked by the browser.
7
Bicep + azd
Provisions the SWA and wires every server-side env var as an app setting; build-time URLs surface as outputs for CI.
8
CI/CD
One GitHub Actions workflow handles main deploys and automatic PR preview environments, cleaned up on merge.
Copilot callout · parallel emission

Scaffold, API, auth config, Bicep, and CI/CD each went out as a single batch of file creates. That's the difference between a nine-step demo and a one-step demo, and it scales across the whole build.

Part 2 · Lessons learned

What's worth carrying into your own builds

  1. 01

    Verify visually, not just by API success

    All four publish calls returned success while charts said "No data." Build a screenshot step into any publish workflow.

  2. 02

    Reuse one validated JSON shape

    Counter widgets are fragile in lvdash.json; bar and text widgets let you reuse a proven pattern across dashboards.

  3. 03

    Genie embed is not anonymous access

    Use a server-side proxy (the /api/genie-chat pattern) for any public or demo deployment.

  4. 04

    VITE_* are never secrets

    Treat them as public the moment you set them: they land in the deployed bundle. Keep tokens in app settings only.

The repeatable pattern

One workflow, proven at two layers of the stack

Step 1

Plan mode

  • Prefix with /plan
  • Agent asks the senior-engineer questions
  • Inspects your live environment
  • You edit & approve before any code
Step 2

Agent Mode

  • Parallel file creates & MCP calls
  • Iterates on real error traces
  • Adapts when an approach is blocked
  • Notebooks & config committed to disk
Step 3

Visible verification

  • Playwright screenshots as ground truth
  • Row-count & FK reconciliation
  • Validate before publish
  • Fix & re-run in seconds
Plan mode is a thinking partner

A vague idea like "build me an F1 analytics app" becomes a fully specified, phased plan, stack decisions, component names, API contracts, security model, all surfaced before a single line of code.

Your turn

  • Bring the same toolchain , GitHub Copilot CLI, the Databricks MCP server, and the AI Dev Kit skills.
  • Go end to end , 60 raw CSVs to a governed lakehouse and a shipped app, at your own pace.
